content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

Look, let’s be super appreciative for a moment to have — at long last — the JavaScript “Temporal” primitive that distinguishes between instants, durations, and timezones. Incredible progress as always in the JavaScript landscape!
Yet this primitive still uses Unix time under-the-hood, the infamous time standard that effectively treats leap seconds as undefined behavior. It’s looking like the race toward IPv6 might beat the race towards a consistent predictable time standard, whether that time standard come to light in two possible ways:

• Switching to TAI (International Atomic Time): TAI measures the physical number of seconds that have elapsed, and so does not need to concern itself with leap seconds. It meets all the constraints one would come to expect, like being monotone (always increasing). However, much of the internet time synchronization still relies on Unix time, and this would be tough to replace.

• Reworking Unix time to be like TAI: There are ongoing efforts to abolish the UTC leap second. The BIPM (International Bureau of Weights and Measures) voted to eliminate leap second adjustments by 2035, which has brought utmost joy to many, though I am skeptical of the potential dangers in reworking an existing format when a transition to a new format might perhaps be safer.
JavaScript’s Role
Every system handles Unix time leap seconds differently:

• Many cloud providers “smear” the leap second adjustment across different periods such as 24 hours.

• Many home operating systems introduce temporary “time travel”, where a leap second is majestically repeated or skipped.
It’s one thing to have a lack of interoperability between systems, but it’s another thing to have no interoperability within the one ecosystem: JavaScript.
The JavaScript standard leaves this leap second behavior up to the whims of the seraphim, and instead chooses to mirror the time arrangements agreed by the overarching environment that the code happens to run in.
I call for JavaScript to introduce a new API that standardizes this, whether that be by exposing a monotonic Unix time via time smearing, or by genuinely implementing the full TAI standard.
Both strategies would be tricky, because the browser would have to correctly translate the system’s time format, or perhaps even implement its own time synchronization system.
But how hard can a custom time server really be?
